title: Constrained Optimization From a Control Perspective via Feedback Linearization

reviews.summary: The paper studies constrained optimization through the lens of nonlinear control via feedback linearization (FL). For equality-constrained problems, it analyzes the continuous-time FL dynamics and proves global convergence to first-order KKT points with an O(1/√T) rate, and shows that a forward-Euler discretization is equivalent to a standard SQP step. Building on this connection, it proposes an FL-based method for inequality constraints and a momentum-accelerated FL variant, providing continuous-time convergence guarantees and illustrative experiments on heterogeneous logistic regression and AC-OPF.
strengths: Establishes a clear and rigorous connection between feedback linearization dynamics and SQP via forward-Euler discretization; this perspective is insightful and ties a classical control technique to a mainstream optimization method.
Extends the FL framework to inequality constraints via a variational/NNLS-style multiplier selection aligned with SQP dual variables and complementary slackness.
Proposes a momentum-accelerated continuous-time FL scheme and provides a Lyapunov-based convergence analysis for the equality-constrained case.
Demonstrates the approach on two representative classes of problems: fairness-style constrained logistic regression and AC optimal power flow on IEEE-39 and IEEE-118 test systems.
Reports KKT-gap trajectories and compares FL-proximal, FL-Newton, and the proposed momentum variant.
The control perspective, the derivation of the FL controller, and the step-by-step construction of the equivalence to SQP are presented in an accessible manner.
Clear statement of assumptions and convergence theorems; the role of exact-penalty Lyapunov functions is well motivated.
Provides global convergence and rates for FL-based constrained optimization, a direction that has largely focused on local behavior to date.
The SQP connection can inform algorithm design choices (e.g., metric T(x)) and suggests principled discretizations and practical implementations.
weaknesses: Assumptions are quite strong: global LICQ (full row rank of J_h(x) everywhere and uniform bounded inverse), global boundedness/Lipschitzness of ∇f and J_h, and higher-order bounded derivatives for momentum analysis. These are unrealistic in many practical constrained problems and limit the scope of the theory.

The inequality-constrained analysis requires bounded multipliers over a prescribed region or feasibility at initialization; existence/uniqueness of the λ-argmin is assumed without verifiable conditions beyond strong LICQ-like properties. The non-smooth ODE is only informally linked to DVI theory.
Momentum analysis proves convergence but not provable acceleration over FL-proximal; conditions on α, K, and coefficients (a1, a2) are conservative and somewhat opaque.

Lacks comparisons with established constrained solvers: augmented Lagrangian/proximal ALM, interior-point methods (e.g., IPOPT), stabilized/modern SQP (S-SQP), projected gradient/primal-dual flows, and other control-inspired flows (e.g., proximal AL gradient flow). The current comparisons are mostly ablations among the proposed variants.
No wall-clock/run-time, robustness, or sensitivity analyses (e.g., conditioning, step-size/η and K selection, scaling with problem size).
The AC-OPF experiments do not benchmark solution quality vs standard solvers and do not report feasibility or optimality gaps to known references.
Some notational inconsistencies and typos (e.g., H vs T, transposes in J_h T J_h^T versus J_h^T T J_h, and occasional missing transposes/signs) hinder precision in a few derivations.
Key proofs are deferred; while acceptable, a brief proof sketch for at least one main result (Theorem 1 or Theorem 2/3) in the main text would improve confidence.

Limited discussion and empirical comparison to primal-dual and augmented Lagrangian flows with global convergence guarantees (e.g., Qu & Li 2018; Ding & Jovanović 2019), interior-point and stabilized SQP literature (Hager 1999, Gill & Robinson 2013, Gill et al. 2017).

Acceleration for constrained optimization beyond Newton/quasi-Newton (e.g., accelerated proximal-gradient under constraints, or momentum in primal-dual methods) is only briefly mentioned; a richer contextualization would clarify novelty.
reviews.questions:
1. Can the global LICQ and global Lipschitz assumptions be relaxed to local versions on an invariant sublevel set (e.g., via descent and bounded trajectories)? What additional arguments would be needed?
2. For the inequality case, can you provide explicit sufficient conditions ensuring uniqueness and boundedness of λ in (11), perhaps via uniform positive definiteness on active sets or Robinson’s CQ?
3. How sensitive is the discretized method to choices of η and K beyond the K = I/η equivalence case? Do you recommend line-search or trust-region globalization to ensure practical robustness?
4. Can the DVI perspective be made rigorous with existence/uniqueness results (e.g., Carathéodory solution) under your assumptions? A short proposition with a cited theorem would help.
5. For momentum, can you characterize problem classes (e.g., affine constraints, quadratic objectives) where an accelerated rate can be proven, even locally? Are there counterexamples where momentum slows convergence?
6. In AC-OPF, how do your solutions compare in optimality and feasibility to established solvers (e.g., IPOPT/SNOPT) and at what computational cost? Could you report wall-clock time and final objective/residuals?
7. Is the FL–SQP equivalence extensible to higher-order discretizations (e.g., trapezoidal/implicit Euler) corresponding to stabilized SQP variants? Could this yield discrete-time convergence guarantees under milder assumptions?
8. How do you envision handling rank-deficient J_h(x) (violation of LICQ) or degeneracy at the solution? Can a stabilized or regularized FL (mirroring S-SQP) be analyzed?
reviews.assessment: This paper offers an appealing and well-articulated control-theoretic perspective on constrained optimization via feedback linearization, delivering global convergence (to first-order KKT) for equality constraints, a clean equivalence to SQP discretizations, an extension to inequality constraints, and a momentum-accelerated continuous-time scheme. The theoretical analyses are technically sound in spirit and the SQP connection is insightful. However, the results depend on strong and somewhat impractical assumptions (global LICQ, globally bounded derivatives), the inequality-case well-posedness is largely assumed rather than established, the momentum analysis does not show acceleration, and the empirical evaluation lacks comparisons to standard constrained optimization baselines. As a result, while the ideas are promising and could influence future work bridging control and optimization, the current submission falls short of the empirical and theoretical robustness expected for ICML. I encourage the authors to (i) relax assumptions via invariant-set arguments, (ii) solidify the DVI well-posedness, (iii) extend theory to discrete-time schemes with globalization, and (iv) substantially broaden experimental comparisons. With these improvements, the contribution could be strong and of high interest to the ICML community.
